html:
[418]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 行脚僧 - Refers to a wandering monk who travels from place to place, often in search of spiritual enlightenment or alms.
  • 独居古刹 - Means 'lives alone in an ancient temple,' describing a solitary and traditional lifestyle.
  • 丐募积有微资 - Means 'has accumulated a small amount of wealth through begging and alms,' indicating a modest financial situation.
  • 以钱易朱提十馀铤 - Means 'exchanged money for over ten bars of gold,' where '朱提' (zhū tí) refers to a type of gold.
  • 铤各五两许 - Means 'each bar weighing about five taels,' where '铤' (dìng) refers to a bar or ingot of metal.
  • 吝惜备至 - Means 'extremely frugal or stingy,' emphasizing the monk's attachment to his wealth.
  • 衣常不周，食常不饱 - Means 'often not well-dressed and often not well-fed,' highlighting the monk's simple and austere lifestyle.
  • 晏如也 - Means 'calm and unperturbed,' indicating the monk's contentment despite his poverty.
  • 晨起啜粥 - Means 'woke up and drank porridge,' describing a simple morning routine.
  • 九华某僧行脚至刹 - Means 'a wandering monk from Jiuhua Mountain arrived at the temple,' where '九华' (jiǔ huá) refers to Jiuhua Mountain, a famous Buddhist site.
  • 饥甚 - Means 'very hungry,' indicating the monk's state of hunger.
  • 出钵乞食 - Means 'took out his begging bowl to ask for food,' a common practice among Buddhist monks.
  • 再三哀求 - Means 'repeatedly pleaded,' emphasizing the urgency of the situation.
  • 啜不顾 - Means 'ignored and did not respond,' indicating the old monk's indifference.
  • 拔戒刀洞老衲胸殪之 - Means 'drew his precept knife and stabbed the old monk in the chest, killing him,' where '戒刀' (jiè dāo) refers to a ceremonial knife used by monks.
  • 饱啖 - Means 'ate heartily,' indicating the satisfaction of the wandering monk after eating.
  • 搜攫其资而遁 - Means 'searched for and took the old monk's wealth and fled,' indicating the theft and escape.
  • 土人见老衲杀死刹中 - Means 'the locals saw the old monk killed in the temple,' indicating the discovery of the crime.
  • 以其无主，瘗而火其刹 - Means 'because there was no owner, they buried him and burned the temple,' indicating the disposal of the body and the temple.
  • 某僧既杀老衲，缠资腰中 - Means 'the wandering monk, after killing the old monk, tied the wealth to his waist,' indicating the theft.
  • 以无人知，意甚得 - Means 'because no one knew, he felt very satisfied,' indicating the monk's sense of security.
  • 腰缠日重 - Means 'the weight around his waist grew heavier,' indicating the increasing burden of the stolen wealth.
  • 几如佩千钧 - Means 'almost like carrying a thousand catties,' where '钧' (jūn) is an ancient unit of weight, indicating the heavy burden.
  • 苦难行走 - Means 'hard to walk,' indicating the difficulty the monk faced due to the weight.
  • 不得已，至深山中，以刀坎地薶其半 - Means 'unable to bear it, he went to a deep mountain and dug a hole to bury half of it,' indicating the monk's attempt to lighten his load.
  • 而重如故 - Means 'but it was still as heavy,' indicating the persistence of the burden.
  • 尽埋乃已 - Means 'he buried it all and then stopped,' indicating the final solution to the problem.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • Wandering monks were common in Chinese Buddhism, often traveling to spread the Dharma and collect alms.
  • Living alone in an ancient temple was a common practice for monks seeking solitude and spiritual growth.
  • Begging and collecting alms was a traditional way for monks to support themselves.
  • A type of gold that was highly valued in ancient China.
  • A bar or ingot of metal, often used for storing wealth.
  • A phrase indicating contentment and calmness, often used to describe a person's demeanor.
  • A simple morning routine that reflects the monk's modest lifestyle.
  • Jiuhua Mountain is a famous Buddhist site in Anhui Province, known for its temples and monasteries.
  • The use of a precept knife by a monk is a rare and extreme action, indicating the severity of the crime.
  • A term indicating hearty eating, often used in a positive context.
  • The act of stealing and fleeing is a common theme in historical and literary works.
  • The discovery of the crime by locals is a typical scenario in historical narratives.
  • The disposal of the body and the temple is a common practice in cases where no owner is known.
  • The theft and the subsequent burden are common themes in stories of crime and punishment.
  • The increasing weight of the stolen wealth is a metaphor for the growing burden of guilt or responsibility.
  • The use of a thousand catties as a metaphor for extreme weight is a common literary device.
  • The difficulty in walking due to the weight is a physical manifestation of the burden.
  • The attempt to lighten the load by burying part of the wealth is a common solution in stories of theft and guilt.
  • The persistence of the burden despite the attempt to lighten it is a common theme in literature.
  • The final solution to the problem is a common resolution in stories of theft and guilt.

Proper Nouns

  • 池阳 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location in China.
  • 九华 - Refers to Jiuhua Mountain, a famous Buddhist site in Anhui Province.
  • 朱提 - A type of gold that was highly valued in ancient China.
  • 铤 - A bar or ingot of metal, often used for storing wealth.
  • 晏如也 - A phrase indicating contentment and calmness.
  • 晨起啜粥 - A simple morning routine.
  • 九华某僧行脚至刹 - A wandering monk from Jiuhua Mountain.
  • 拔戒刀洞老衲胸殪之 - The act of drawing a precept knife and stabbing the old monk.
  • 饱啖 - A term indicating hearty eating.
  • 搜攫其资而遁 - The act of stealing and fleeing.
  • 土人见老衲杀死刹中 - The discovery of the crime.

[419]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 每日暮 - Literally 'every evening,' setting the time frame for the events.
  • 老衲 - A humble term for a monk, used here to refer to the monk who appears to the narrator.
  • 口宣佛号 - Means 'reciting the Buddha's name out loud.'
  • 阿弥陀佛 - The name of Amitabha Buddha, often recited as a prayer or invocation.
  • 喃喃不休 - Describes the continuous, low murmuring of the monk's recitation.
  • 心恶之 - Means 'to dislike it in one's heart,' expressing the narrator's inner aversion.
  • 冤孽难解 - Refers to unresolved karmic debts or grudges that are hard to resolve.
  • 计朝菩萨 - Means 'to think of the Bodhisattva,' suggesting a turning to Buddhist deities for help.
  • 忏悔 - Means 'to repent' or 'to atone for sins.'
  • 五台、峨眉、南海 - Refers to the Five Great Mountains of Buddhism (Wutai, Emei, and Nanshan among them), which are sacred sites.
  • 灵山 - Literally 'spirit mountain,' a term often used to refer to sacred mountains or Buddhist sites.
  • 顶礼求忏 - Means 'to prostrate oneself in prayer and seek atonement.'
  • 无验 - Means 'no effect' or 'no results,' indicating that the narrator's efforts to repent were unsuccessful.
  • 无可如何 - Means 'helpless' or 'powerless,' expressing the narrator's resignation.
  • 九华 - Refers to Jiuhua Mountain, a famous Buddhist site in Anhui Province.
  • 上天门 - Literally 'the gate to heaven,' a metaphorical term for a high or sacred place.
  • 惨然色变 - Means 'to change color with fear,' describing the narrator's sudden fear.
  • 自挝其胸 - Means 'to strike one's own chest,' a gesture of self-reproach or remorse.
  • 此事何能瞒人 - Means 'how could this be hidden from people,' expressing the narrator's sense of inevitability.
  • 我死晚矣 - Means 'I will die later,' indicating the narrator's acceptance of his impending death.
  • 耸身一跃 - Means 'to leap suddenly,' describing the narrator's final act.
  • 崖下适有枯枝 - Means 'a dry branch happened to be below the cliff,' describing the fortuitous circumstance.
  • 贯胸而死 - Means 'to die with the branch piercing through the chest,' describing the manner of the narrator's death.
  • 咋舌骇叹 - Means 'to gasp in horror and sigh,' describing the reaction of the onlookers.
  • 佥合十作偈 - Means 'all joined their hands and composed a verse,' describing the collective response of the onlookers.
  • 孽由自作 - Means 'karma is self-created,' a Buddhist principle.
  • 信不可活 - Means 'belief cannot save,' emphasizing the futility of faith in this context.
  • 凡我众生 - Means 'all sentient beings,' a broad reference to humanity.
  • 鉴此行脚 - Means 'learn from this wanderer,' referring to the narrator's journey and fate.
  • 吝粥一勺 - Means 'to be stingy with a single spoon of porridge,' referring to a past act of kindness or lack thereof.
  • 甘丧躯壳 - Means 'willingly lose one's body,' indicating the narrator's acceptance of his fate.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 阿弥陀佛 - A central figure in Mahayana Buddhism, associated with the Western Pure Land.
  • 五台、峨眉、南海 - These are the Five Great Mountains of Buddhism, each associated with different Buddhist deities and practices.
  • 灵山 - A term often used to refer to sacred mountains or Buddhist sites, emphasizing their spiritual significance.
  • 顶礼求忏 - A common Buddhist practice of prostrating oneself in prayer and seeking atonement for sins.
  • 九华 - Jiuhua Mountain, a famous Buddhist site in Anhui Province, associated with the Bodhisattva Ksitigarbha.
  • 上天门 - A metaphorical term for a high or sacred place, often used in Buddhist contexts.
  • 偈 - A short verse or poem, often used in Buddhist contexts to express spiritual truths.
  • 行脚 - A term used to describe the wandering of monks or spiritual seekers, often associated with Buddhist practice.
  • 吝粥一勺 - Refers to a past act of kindness or lack thereof, emphasizing the consequences of one's actions in Buddhist karma.

Proper Nouns

  • 阿弥陀佛 - The name of Amitabha Buddha.
  • 五台 - Wutai Mountain, one of the Five Great Mountains of Buddhism.
  • 峨眉 - Emei Mountain, one of the Five Great Mountains of Buddhism.
  • 南海 - Nanshan Mountain, one of the Five Great Mountains of Buddhism.
  • 九华 - Jiuhua Mountain, a famous Buddhist site in Anhui Province.
  • 上天门 - A metaphorical term for a high or sacred place.
  • 行脚 - A term used to describe the wandering of monks or spiritual seekers.

[420]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 财之为祟大矣哉 - Rhetorical expression meaning 'How great is the calamity caused by wealth!' Emphasizes the severe consequences of wealth.
  • 行脚攫资 - Literally 'traveling and seizing wealth,' referring to monks or travelers who accumulate wealth through various means.
  • 坐此结怨成冤 - Means 'to beget enmity and grievances,' indicating the negative consequences of accumulating wealth.
  • 合十两偈 - Means 'to join hands and recite two verses,' a common Buddhist gesture and practice.
  • 当头棒喝 - Zen Buddhist term meaning 'a sudden, direct shock to the mind,' used to break through spiritual delusions.
  • 凡我众生 - Means 'all of us sentient beings,' emphasizing a universal appeal.
  • 从善毋恶 - Means 'to follow good and avoid evil,' a moral exhortation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 里乘子 - Fictional or historical figure, possibly a monk or sage, who provides commentary on the text.
  • 老衲 - Term used by monks to refer to themselves, meaning 'old monk.'
  • 某明府 - Refers to a local official, specifically a county magistrate, with '明府' being a respectful title for a local official.
  • 当涂令 - Position of county magistrate in the county of Dangtu.
  • 某明府以进士宰当涂 - Indicates that the official was appointed as the county magistrate of Dangtu after passing the imperial examination.
  • 某履任，恰值大水 - Describes the official's arrival coinciding with a major flood.
  • 官庖突烟垂绝 - Means 'the fire in the official kitchen is about to go out,' indicating extreme poverty and lack of resources.
  • 饥民日塞署前 - Describes the situation where starving people gather outside the government office.
  • 某书空嗟叹 - Means 'the official sighs and laments,' indicating his helplessness.
  • 幕友 - Refers to the official's advisors or assistants.
  • 据实上状请赈 - Means 'to report the truth and request relief,' indicating the official's plan to seek aid.
  • 大吏必许 - Means 'the higher officials will surely approve,' indicating the expected outcome of the request.
  • 帑金 - Refers to government funds.
  • 半侵肥己槖 - Means 'to embezzle half of the funds for personal gain,' indicating corruption.
  • 纪纲人等 - Refers to the officials and staff of the government office.

Proper Nouns

  • 里乘子 - Fictional or historical figure, possibly a monk or sage.
  • 老衲 - Term used by monks to refer to themselves.
  • 某明府 - Local official, specifically a county magistrate.
  • 当涂令 - Position of county magistrate in the county of Dangtu.
  • 某明府以进士宰当涂 - Official was appointed as the county magistrate of Dangtu after passing the imperial examination.
  • 某履任，恰值大水 - Official's arrival coincided with a major flood.
  • 官庖突烟垂绝 - Fire in the official kitchen is about to go out.
  • 饥民日塞署前 - Starving people gather outside the government office.
  • 某书空嗟叹 - Official sighs and laments.
  • 幕友 - Official's advisors or assistants.
  • 据实上状请赈 - Report the truth and request relief.
  • 大吏必许 - Higher officials will surely approve.
  • 帑金 - Government funds.
  • 半侵肥己槖 - Embezzle half of the funds for personal gain.
  • 纪纲人等 - Officials and staff of the government office.

[421]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 视之莫如也 - Ambiguous expression meaning 'look at it and there is nothing better' or 'look at it and it is unmatched,' suggesting a sense of superiority or excellence.
  • 亡何 - Classical phrase meaning 'soon' or 'not long after,' indicating a short period of time.
  • 吞蚀赈金 - Literal meaning 'to swallow and erode relief funds,' referring to the misuse or embezzlement of charitable donations.
  • 耗费无存 - Meaning 'all spent and nothing left,' indicating complete depletion.
  • 扶榇回籍 - Literal meaning 'to carry the coffin back to one's hometown,' referring to the act of returning a deceased person's body to their home.
  • 典质既罄 - Meaning 'all collateral has been exhausted,' referring to the complete depletion of assets used as security for loans.
  • 藉针黹聊以糊口 - Literal meaning 'relying on sewing to barely make ends meet,' referring to a meager living through needlework.
  • 觉刺绣又不如倚市门矣 - Literal meaning 'feeling that embroidery is no longer as good as standing by the market gate,' suggesting a shift to a more desperate means of survival.
  • 道光丙午 - Refers to the year 1836 during the Daoguang era of the Qing dynasty.
  • 德化万鳌峰夫子 - Refers to a respected scholar or official named Wan Aofeng from the county of Dehua.
  • 招予幕府 - Meaning 'invited me to his office or entourage.'
  • 邑绅蔡翁 - Refers to a local gentry named Cai, an elder or senior figure.
  • 招饮 - Meaning 'invited to a banquet or drinking session.'
  • 某之雏女甫破瓜 - Literal meaning 'the young daughter of someone just turned sixteen,' referring to a girl who has just reached adulthood (the age of 16).
  • 艳而善歌 - Meaning 'beautiful and skilled at singing,' describing the girl's appearance and talents.
  • 将召侑觞 - Meaning 'planning to invite her to entertain at the banquet.'
  • 君休矣 - A polite way of saying 'you stop' or 'you desist,' used to advise against an action.
  • 此女流荡不偶 - Meaning 'this woman has been wandering and has had bad luck,' suggesting a life of hardship and misfortune.
  • 固乃翁居官作孽使然 - Meaning 'it is certainly because your father-in-law's actions as an official were wicked,' attributing the woman's fate to her father-in-law's corrupt behavior.
  • 物伤其类 - Phrase meaning 'grief for one's kind,' expressing sympathy for those in similar situations.
  • 小子虽不肖 - Meaning 'I, though unworthy,' a humble self-reference.
  • 实不忍与见也 - Meaning 'truly cannot bear to see her,' expressing a strong aversion to witnessing the woman's situation.
  • 蔡点首曰 - Meaning 'Cai nodded and said,' indicating agreement.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 道光丙午 - Refers to the year 1836 during the Daoguang era of the Qing dynasty.
  • 德化万鳌峰夫子 - Refers to a respected scholar or official named Wan Aofeng from the county of Dehua.
  • 邑绅蔡翁 - Refers to a local gentry named Cai, an elder or senior figure.
  • 某之雏女甫破瓜 - Refers to a young girl who has just turned sixteen, a traditional age marking the transition to womanhood.
  • 艳而善歌 - Describes the girl's beauty and singing skills, common attributes used to describe women in classical literature.
  • 将召侑觞 - Refers to the practice of inviting a woman to entertain at a banquet, a common social custom in ancient China.
  • 君休矣 - A polite way of advising against an action, reflecting the social norms of addressing superiors or peers respectfully.
  • 此女流荡不偶 - Suggests a life of hardship and misfortune, reflecting the social and economic conditions of the time.
  • 固乃翁居官作孽使然 - Attributes the woman's fate to her father-in-law's corrupt behavior, reflecting the belief in the moral consequences of one's actions.
  • 物伤其类 - Expresses sympathy for those in similar situations, a common sentiment in classical literature.
  • 小子虽不肖 - A humble self-reference, reflecting the cultural emphasis on modesty and humility.
  • 实不忍与见也 - Expresses a strong aversion to witnessing the woman's situation, reflecting the moral and ethical values of the time.
  • 蔡点首曰 - Indicates agreement, reflecting the social norms of nodding and verbal confirmation.

Proper Nouns

  • 道光丙午 - The year 1836 during the Daoguang era of the Qing dynasty.
  • 德化万鳌峰夫子 - Wan Aofeng, a respected scholar or official from the county of Dehua.
  • 邑绅蔡翁 - Cai, a local gentry and elder figure.
  • 某之雏女 - The young daughter of someone, referred to as 'her' in the text.
  • 艳而善歌 - A description of the girl's appearance and talents.
  • 将召侑觞 - A specific action planned by Cai.
  • 君休矣 - A polite way of advising against an action.
  • 此女流荡不偶 - A specific description of the woman's situation.
  • 固乃翁居官作孽使然 - A specific reference to the woman's father-in-law's corrupt behavior.
  • 物伤其类 - A specific phrase expressing sympathy for those in similar situations.
  • 小子虽不肖 - A specific humble self-reference.
  • 实不忍与见也 - A specific expression of aversion to witnessing the woman's situation.
  • 蔡点首曰 - A specific indication of agreement.

[422]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 挟资甚丰 - Carrying a large amount of money or wealth.
  • 紫面阔颡，髯丛如蝟 - Describes the appearance of the guest with a purple face, broad forehead, and thick beard like a hedgehog.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 巡抚 - A high-ranking official in the Qing dynasty responsible for administrative and judicial duties in a province.
  • 黔南 - Refers to the southern part of the province of Guizhou, historically known for its diverse ethnic groups and rugged terrain.
  • 乡试 - One of the three levels of the imperial examination system, held every three years in provincial capitals.
  • 榜人 - Boatmen or steersmen were essential in river transport during the Qing dynasty, often trusted by travelers.

Proper Nouns

  • 巡抚 - A title for a high-ranking official in the Qing dynasty.
  • 黔南 - A historical region in southern China.
  • 乡试 - The provincial examination in the imperial examination system.
  • 榜人 - A term for boatmen or steersmen.
  • 紫面阔颡 - Descriptive terms for physical appearance.
  • 髯丛如蝟 - Descriptive terms for physical appearance.

[423]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 拱手加额 - A traditional gesture of respect in Chinese culture, combining a hand salute with a forehead bow.
  • 谢公子高义 - Expresses gratitude to the young master for his noble conduct.
  • 并叩邦族 - Acknowledges the young master's family and social status.
  • 公子以告 - The young master informed someone about the encounter.
  • 客敛容致敬曰 - The guest, showing respect, said...
  • 固是贵公子 - Indeed, you are a noble young master.
  • 小人不知，失礼勿怪 - I was unaware of your status, please forgive my rudeness.
  • 自言姓李、行二，江西人 - He introduced himself as Li, the second son from Jiangxi.
  • 他日舟过敝境，当少伸地主之敬，幸勿见却 - If we meet again, I will show you the hospitality of a host, please don't refuse.
  • 公子颔之 - The young master nodded in agreement.
  • 见李语言豪爽 - The young master found Li's speech to be bold and straightforward.
  • 试与谈史事，颇熟，意非庸流 - When they talked about history, Li was very knowledgeable, indicating he was not an ordinary person.
  • 每饭必招与俱，李亦不辞，且豪于饮 - The young master would invite Li to eat with him, and Li never refused, and he enjoyed drinking.
  • 醉后论古今成败得失，尤娓娓可听 - After getting drunk, they would discuss the successes and failures of ancient and modern times, which was particularly engaging to listen to.
  • 公子大乐，相见恨晚 - The young master was very pleased and wished they had met earlier.
  • 一日薄暮，舟泊村市，李携白金一笏登岸 - One evening, when the boat stopped at a village market, Li took a piece of silver and went ashore.
  • 公子笑问：“何为？” - The young master smiled and asked, 'What for?'
  • 李笑曰：“日扰公子，殊切愧恧，将薄市酒脯，聊酬万一。” - Li laughed and said, 'I have been bothering you, I feel very ashamed, I will buy some wine and meat to slightly repay you.'
  • 公子摇手止之，李不顾，掉头径去 - The young master waved his hand to stop him, but Li ignored him and walked away directly.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 拱手加额 - A traditional gesture of respect in Chinese culture.
  • 谢公子高义 - Expressing gratitude for noble behavior, a common practice in feudal society.
  • 并叩邦族 - Acknowledging the social status and family of the young master, reflecting the importance of lineage and social hierarchy.
  • 公子 - Refers to a young man of noble birth, often used in literature to denote a young noble or scholar.
  • 江西人 - Indicates that Li is from Jiangxi province, which is historically significant in Chinese culture.
  • 酒脯 - Refers to wine and meat, traditional offerings or gifts in Chinese culture.
  • 薄市 - A small market, often found in rural areas, where local goods are sold.
  • 白金一笏 - A piece of silver,笏 (hù) is a tablet used by officials in ancient China, here it refers to a piece of silver.
  • 日扰公子 - Refers to the daily interactions and hospitality provided by the young master.
  • 殊切愧恧 - Expresses deep shame and regret, a common sentiment in Chinese literature when someone feels indebted or guilty.
  • 聊酬万一 - Slightly repaying a debt, indicating that Li wants to make a small gesture of gratitude.
  • 公子颔之 - Nodding in agreement, a non-verbal sign of approval and acceptance.
  • 见李语言豪爽 - Describes Li's bold and straightforward manner of speaking, a characteristic often admired in Chinese culture.
  • 试与谈史事，颇熟，意非庸流 - Indicates that Li is well-versed in history, suggesting he is not an ordinary person.
  • 每饭必招与俱，李亦不辞，且豪于饮 - Describes the close relationship and mutual respect between the young master and Li.
  • 醉后论古今成败得失，尤娓娓可听 - Highlights the intellectual and engaging nature of their conversations.
  • 公子大乐，相见恨晚 - Expresses the young master's joy and regret at not meeting Li earlier.
  • 一日薄暮，舟泊村市，李携白金一笏登岸 - Sets the scene of a peaceful evening in a rural market.
  • 公子笑问：“何为？” - A light-hearted question, showing the young master's curiosity and friendliness.
  • 李笑曰：“日扰公子，殊切愧恧，将薄市酒脯，聊酬万一。” - Li's response shows his gratitude and desire to repay the young master.
  • 公子摇手止之，李不顾，掉头径去 - The young master's gesture of refusal and Li's departure, indicating a moment of tension or misunderstanding.

Proper Nouns

  • 公子 - A young man of noble birth.
  • 李 - The guest's surname.
  • 江西 - A province in southern China, historically significant in Chinese culture.
  • 酒脯 - Traditional offerings or gifts in Chinese culture.
  • 薄市 - A small market, often found in rural areas.
  • 白金一笏 - A piece of silver,笏 (hù) is a tablet used by officials in ancient China, here it refers to a piece of silver.

[424]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 公子窃命仆展视襆被 - Formal expression meaning 'the young master secretly ordered the servant to unfold the wrapped bundle.'
  • 内一匕首，长尺有半，莹然如雪，寒光逼人 - Describes the contents of the bundle: a dagger, one and a half feet long, shining like snow with a cold, piercing light.
  • 又腊干人手一具，馀无他物 - Indicates that there is also a dried human hand, and nothing else.
  • 公子惊异 - Means 'the young master was astonished.'
  • 急命依旧束好 - Means 'he quickly ordered it to be wrapped up again.'
  • 意李非侠客即盗魁 - Means 'he thought Li was either a knight-errant or a bandit leader.'
  • 心窃自危 - Means 'he secretly felt uneasy.'
  • 计惟以礼款之，以德结之，或可相感 - Means 'he decided to treat him with courtesy and kindness, hoping it might win his favor.'

  • 李袖出小旗一角授公子 - '袖出' means 'to take out from one's sleeve', and '授公子' means 'to give to the young lord'. Describes a gesture where '李强' discreetly gives a small flag to the young lord.
  • 令善藏之 - Means 'to instruct to keep it well', advising the young lord to store the flag carefully.
  • 李作色曰 - '作色' means 'to change expression' or 'to look stern', indicating that '李强' became serious or angry.
  • 公以小人之物为盗泉耶 - '公' is a respectful term for 'you', '小人之物' means 'something of a lowly person', and '盗泉' refers to a place where water was believed to corrupt the drinker. A rhetorical question implying that the young lord is treating the gift as if it were tainted.
  • 盗泉去贪泉几何 - '贪泉' is another place believed to corrupt the drinker. This phrase asks how far '盗泉' is from '贪泉', suggesting that both are equally corrupting.
  • 如恐为盗泉所污 - Means 'if you fear being corrupted by '盗泉'', continuing the metaphor.
  • 则公囊中所有，亦未必果皆廉泉也 - '廉泉' refers to a place where water was believed to cleanse the drinker. This phrase suggests that if the young lord fears corruption, then the contents of his bag might not all be pure either.
  • 公子以李言激烈，只合拜受 - '激烈' means 'strong' or 'forceful', and '拜受' means 'to accept with a bow'. Shows that the young lord found '李强's words strong and decided to accept the gift with a bow.
  • 李坚订后约 - '坚订' means 'firmly agreed', and '后约' means 'a future agreement'. Shows that '李强' insisted on a future meeting.
  • 嘱毕，仍命肩舆送公子归舟 - '嘱毕' means 'after the instructions', '肩舆' means 'a sedan chair', and '送公子归舟' means 'to send the young lord back to the boat'. Shows that after giving the instructions, '李强' had the young lord escorted back to his boat.
  • 道路坦夷，不似旧径 - '坦夷' means 'smooth and level', and '不似旧径' means 'not like the old path'. Suggests that the road taken was different from the usual one.
  • 昧爽始达舟次 - '昧爽' means 'dawn', and '始达舟次' means 'finally reached the boat'. Indicates that they arrived at the boat early in the morning.
  • 舟人见公子，皆大欢喜，佥谓公子去后，李遣人逻守维谨 - '舟人' means 'boatmen', '大欢喜' means 'great joy', '佥谓' means 'all said', '李遣人逻守' means 'sent people to guard', and '维谨' means 'very carefully'. Shows that the boatmen were very happy to see the young lord and mentioned that '李强' had sent people to guard the area carefully after the young lord left.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 少选饭熟，舟人但自饱啖，并不食客 - This sentence is elliptical and must be read as a single statement about the boatmen's behavior.
  • 无何，夕阳西沈，诸客饥焰中烧，颇不能耐 - This sequence describes the progression of events, from the sun setting to the guests' increasing hunger.
  • 舟子忽率其党各执刀械进前，厉声谓诸客曰 - This sentence introduces the sudden and threatening action of the boatmen.
  • 汝等所挟资可速献出，我当为善藏之。不则恐有不虞，悔无及也 - This clause presents the boatmen's demand and the threat of consequences.
  • 我等既同一舟，患难自当与共。各挟薄资固在舟中，纵有不虞，亦全仗主人防卫，如可免患，不惜重酬 - This clause is a plea by the guests, emphasizing their unity and reliance on the boatmen.
  • 李依少年傍，亦窃自危 - This sentence describes the narrator's position and feelings of unease.
  • 第见少年从容起立，喝谓舟子曰 - This sentence introduces the narrator's intervention, with the young man standing up and addressing the boatmen.
  • 汝休孟浪！汝抑知步家 - This clause is a direct challenge to the boatmen, with the young man questioning their knowledge of something (possibly a name or a term).

  • 自称无知冒犯，罪该万死，愿出资另为买舟，载众至沪，以求赎罪。 - A statement of self-admission and plea for forgiveness, meaning 'they admitted to being ignorant and having committed a grave offense, deserving death, and were willing to pay money to buy another boat to take everyone to Shanghai to atone for their sins,' where '自称' means 'admitted,' '无知冒犯' means 'ignorant and having committed a grave offense,' '罪该万死' means 'deserving death,' '愿出资另为买舟' means 'willing to pay money to buy another boat,' '载众至沪' means 'to take everyone to Shanghai,' and '以求赎罪' means 'to atone for their sins.'
  • 汝既知罪，姑暂宽贷。 - A statement of temporary leniency, meaning 'Since you know your crime, I will temporarily forgive you,' where '汝既知罪' means 'since you know your crime' and '姑暂宽贷' means 'I will temporarily forgive you.'

  • 至沪后，同人佥德少年，谋有以报，少年悉笑而却之。 - A statement of gratitude and refusal, meaning 'After arriving in Shanghai, everyone praised the young man, planning to repay him, but the young man laughed and refused,' where '至沪后' means 'after arriving in Shanghai,' '同人佥德少年' means 'everyone praised the young man,' '谋有以报' means 'planning to repay him,' and '少年悉笑而却之' means 'the young man laughed and refused.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '其子聞有父書，喜出叩見，問：『父書何在？』' is clear in its structure, but the emotional state of the son ('喜出') adds a layer of meaning that needs to be conveyed in the translation.
  • The father's response '生笑撫其背曰：『兒不知耶？我即汝父是也。』' is a direct confirmation of his identity, but the use of '笑撫其背' (laughing and patting his back) adds a layer of affection and reassurance.
  • The son's reaction '其子驚喜猶疑，生窺其意，謂曰：『兒如不信，可呼汝母出見，自能知之。』' is a mix of surprise and doubt, and the father's suggestion to bring the mother out adds a layer of verification.
  • The mother's response '其母聞之，喜謂其子曰：『果兒父也。』' confirms the father's identity, but the phrase '果兒父也' (indeed, he is your father) adds a layer of certainty.
  • The sentence '蓋生所云，乃當日推磨時相諧之詞，非他人所與知也。' explains that the phrase '推磨' was a private joke or a way to confirm identity, but the exact meaning is unclear without further context.

  • 以充缠头之费 - Means 'to use as payment for courtesans.' '缠头' is a term for money given to courtesans.
  • 夜漏三下 - Means 'at the third watch of the night,' indicating a very late hour.
  • 瞷戴睡熟 - Means 'waited until Dai was asleep.' '瞷' means 'to spy on' or 'to wait for.'
  • 遽剁戴首于枕 - Means 'quickly chopped off Dai's head on the pillow.' '遽' means 'quickly' or 'suddenly.'
  • 未殊 - Means 'not yet dead' or 'still alive.'
  • 手颤几难举刃 - Means 'his hands were trembling, making it difficult to lift the blade.'
  • 榜人闻声起窥 - Means 'the boatman heard the sound and got up to look.'
  • 大惊欲呼 - Means 'was greatly alarmed and wanted to call out.'
  • 甲乙遽掩其口劫之曰 - Means 'Jia and Yi quickly covered his mouth and threatened him.' '劫' means 'to threaten.'
  • 声则杀汝 - Means 'if you make a sound, we will kill you.'
  • 榜人禁骇不敢复声 - Means 'the boatman was terrified and dared not make a sound.'
  • 舁尸投诸河 - Means 'carried the body and threw it into the river.' '舁' means 'to carry on a stretcher.'
  • 启囊各瓜分六百金 - Means 'opened the bag and divided the 600 taels of silver equally.' '瓜分' means 'to divide equally.'
  • 以百金并衣物犒榜人 - Means 'gave 100 taels of silver and clothes as a reward to the boatman.'
  • 又假戴手书寄小妻某氏 - Means 'also forged a letter from Dai to send to his concubine, Miss So-and-so.' '假' means 'to forge.'
  • 谓甲乙伺应无状，途次业俱屏逐，如来见汝，切勿再留为要 - Means 'stating that Jia and Yi had behaved improperly and were already dismissed, and if she saw them, she should not keep them any longer.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 戴明府 - Refers to a local official named Dai, a common title for magistrates in ancient China.
  • 小妻 - Refers to a concubine, a secondary wife in a polygamous household.
  • 清江 - Refers to Qingjiang, a place name in China.
  • 白金 - Refers to silver, a valuable metal used as currency.
  • 缠头 - Refers to money given to courtesans, a common practice in ancient China.
  • 榜人 - Refers to the boatman or crew of a boat.
  • 夜漏三下 - Refers to the third watch of the night, a specific time in the traditional Chinese time system.

Proper Nouns

  • 清江 - The place where the concubine was left.
  • 甲乙 - The names of the servants who committed the crime.
  • 榜人 - The boatman or crew of the boat.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 督率 - Highlights the traditional role of elders in overseeing household affairs, particularly in managing the labor of younger family members.
  • 作苦 - Emphasizes the hard work and toil that was often expected in traditional Chinese households, especially in rural settings.

  • 一方耆宿 - Refers to a respected elder in a particular region, often someone who is well-known and highly regarded in their community.

  • 令亲 - A respectful term used to refer to someone's relatives, often in a formal or polite context.

  • 名诸生 - Refers to a well-known scholar or student, often someone who has achieved recognition in their field of study.
  • 中副本 - Refers to passing the supplementary examination in the imperial examination system, which was a lower-tier exam compared to the main imperial examinations.
  • 仅生二女 - Reflects the traditional Chinese preference for male children, as having only daughters was often seen as less desirable.
  • 幼即教其读书 - Highlights the importance of education in traditional Chinese culture, where children were often educated in reading and writing from a young age.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 然则据君所言，谓非君杀吾妹而何？” - Rhetorical question is a challenge to the listener, implying that the only logical conclusion is that the listener killed the speaker's sister.
  • 乃叹曰：“今而后我知之矣。大约家君恐小生疾不能起，故商之阿舅，换羽移宫，可谓弥缝甚巧。 - Complex explanation of the father's actions, using metaphors and circumlocutions to describe a clever plan.
  • 但卿年齿较所拜甲子差长，面貌较初见时微丰，且谛视颊无微涡，不无少疑。 - Lists several observations that cast doubt on the identity of the person being addressed.
  • 然难弟难兄，亦何酷肖若此！” - Rhetorical question expresses surprise at the similarity between the two people, despite the doubts raised.
  • 女曰：“妾姊妹面貌相肖，多疑孪生，即吾母有时亦几误认。今即若此，将焉置吾妹也？” - The female speaker explains that her sisters look very similar and are often mistaken for twins, even by their mother, and asks what to do about the situation.
  • 公子笑揖女曰：“卿妹几杀小生，赖卿救之；小生几杀卿妹，亦非卿不能救之。二人之命，生死惟卿。 - The male speaker acknowledges the near-fatal situation and expresses gratitude for the female speaker's intervention, emphasizing the critical role she played.

  • 为司书记 - He served as a secretary, describing his position.
  • 慕姬名 - He admired the courtesan's reputation, indicating his interest in her.
  • 厚贽往见 - He brought a substantial gift to visit her, describing his visit.
  • 鸨媪导至曲房 - The madam led him to a secluded room, describing the setting of the meeting.
  • 令坐少待 - She asked him to sit and wait for a moment, indicating the initial interaction.
  • 久之，两婢扶姬出 - After a while, two maids helped the courtesan come out, describing the courtesan's appearance.
  • 姬时小极新瘥 - The courtesan had just recovered from a minor illness, indicating her recent health status.
  • 蓬首裹青绡帕 - With her hair loose and wrapped in a green silk scarf, describing her appearance.
  • 缟衣素裳 - Wearing a white robe and a plain skirt, describing her attire.
  • 绝无修饰 - Completely unadorned, emphasizing her simplicity.
  • 而黑发白肉，明眸皓齿 - With black hair, white skin, bright eyes, and white teeth, describing her physical features.
  • 光彩倍觉照人 - Her radiance was even more noticeable, emphasizing her beauty.
  • 展问邦族 - She asked about his family and background, indicating the conversation.
  • 彼此略一酬对 - They exchanged a few polite words, describing the interaction.
  • 意甚相洽 - They got along very well, indicating their mutual liking.
  • 日昳 - When the sun was high in the afternoon, indicating the time of day.
  • 生欲兴辞 - The scholar wanted to take his leave, indicating his intention to depart.
  • 姬笑止之曰 - The courtesan smiled and stopped him, saying, describing her response.
  • 君去何太急也 - Why are you leaving so quickly? Indicating her surprise at his haste.
  • 如不嫌仓卒主人 - If you do not mind the hurried hospitality of the host, indicating her offer.
  • 即屈晚餐何如 - Would you stay for dinner? Indicating her invitation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 雄黄 - A mineral form of mercury sulfide, often used in traditional Chinese medicine and alchemy, believed to have protective and medicinal properties.
  • 山川灵气 - Refers to the belief in traditional Chinese culture that natural elements like mountains and rivers possess spiritual energy.
  • 三千年 - A hyperbolic expression indicating a very long time, emphasizing the rarity and value of the cinnabar.
  • 维扬 - An old name for Yangzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province, known for its cultural and economic significance.
  • 有名妓 - Refers to a famous courtesan, a woman skilled in music, poetry, and conversation, often serving as a companion to wealthy men.
  • 楚北 - Refers to the northern part of the state of Chu, an ancient Chinese state.
  • 世家子 - Refers to a person from an old noble family, indicating a high social status.
  • 父执 - Refers to a father's friend or colleague, often a person of high social standing.
  • 司书记 - Refers to a secretary, a position responsible for administrative tasks.
  • 厚贽 - Refers to a substantial gift, often brought as a sign of respect or to gain favor.
  • 鸨媪 - Refers to the madam of a brothel, responsible for managing the courtesans.
  • 曲房 - Refers to a secluded room, often used for private meetings.
  • 蓬首 - Refers to hair left loose and unbound, often associated with simplicity and modesty.
  • 青绡帕 - Refers to a green silk scarf, a decorative item.
  • 缟衣素裳 - Refers to a white robe and a plain skirt, indicating a simple and elegant attire.
  • 明眸皓齿 - Refers to bright eyes and white teeth, describing physical beauty.
  • 日昳 - Refers to the time of day when the sun is high in the afternoon, indicating a specific moment.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 饭毕 - After the meal, a common phrase indicating the completion of a meal.
  • 姬命婢秉烛导生入己室 - The lady ordered a maid to hold a candle and lead the scholar into her room, using a formal structure typical of Classical Chinese.
  • 室中奁具之外，琴书图画，罗列满案 - Outside the dressing case in the room, musical instruments, books, paintings, and calligraphy were arranged on the table, a common way to describe items displayed on a surface.
  • 生故善琴 - The scholar was skilled at playing the qin (a traditional Chinese zither), a concise way to express proficiency.
  • 朱弦玉轸，装潢精洁 - The strings were red, the tuning pegs were jade, and the decoration was refined and clean, describing the high quality of the instrument.
  • 随手挑拨，其声清越异常 - With a casual touch, the sound was exceptionally clear and resonant, emphasizing the ease with which the scholar played.
  • 君亦解此耶 - Do you also understand this? A polite way to inquire about someone's knowledge.
  • 此琴甚古，卿从何处得来 - This qin is very old. Where did you get it? Highlights the age of the instrument.
  • 此卞玉京物也 - This belongs to Bian Yujing, a historical figure known for her beauty and talent.
  • 鹾贾出兼金购以见贻 - A salt merchant paid a high price to obtain it and presented it to me, indicating the value placed on the instrument.
  • 君不见腹有款识耶 - Have you not seen the inscription on the belly of the qin? Refers to the markings or inscriptions on the instrument.
  • 生视琴腹镌隶书“四善”二字 - The scholar saw the two characters '四善' (Four Virtues) engraved in clerical script on the belly of the qin, a reference to moral and ethical values.
  • 款署“乙酉春三月玉京道人”九字 - The inscription reads 'Yujing Taoist in the third month of the Year of the Rooster,' providing a specific date and the name of the person who inscribed the qin.
  • 生阅之，极为称赞 - The scholar read it and highly praised it, indicating strong approval.
  • 卿蓄此，必是高手 - You must be a master to have such a qin, acknowledging the lady's skill and the value of the qin.
  • 请试一挥，愿为洗耳 - Please play one piece, and I will listen attentively, a polite way to request attention.
  • 久未弄此，今既遇赏音，不敢藏拙 - I haven't played it in a long time, but now that I have met someone who appreciates music, I dare not hide my clumsiness, a modest way to acknowledge one's abilities.
  • 倘有舛误，乞赐指点 - If there are any mistakes, please give me guidance, a formal way to request feedback.
  • 高卷翠袖，轻舒玉腕 - She raised her green sleeves high and gracefully extended her white wrists, poetic descriptions of her movements.
  • 为谱《合欢》之词 - She composed a piece called 'Héhuān' (Joyful Union), a common theme in classical Chinese poetry and music.
  • 君何翩翩兮妾何娟娟 - How graceful you are, and how beautiful I am, poetic adjectives.
  • 未见兮心相怜，既见兮心相欢 - Before meeting, our hearts were fond of each other; after meeting, our hearts were joyful, expressing emotional connection.
  • 花合欢兮并蒂长眷，人合欢兮如意延年 - Flowers unite in joy, growing together in eternal affection; people unite in joy, wishing for a long life, poetic expressions of love and longevity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 款署“乙酉春三月玉京道人”九字 - Provides a specific date and the name of the person who inscribed the qin, adding historical context.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 生闻而喜 - The young man heard and was pleased.
  • 两人同声称感 - Both of them cried out in gratitude.
  • 夙夜必偕 - They were together day and night.
  • 殆有终焉之志 - Almost had the intention of making it their final resting place.
  • 客来，姬概谢不见 - When guests came, Ji generally declined to see them.
  • 媪以生为太守客 - The old woman regarded the young man as a guest of the governor.
  • 多不敢与校 - Most dared not argue with her.
  • 亡何，太守内擢京卿 - Not long after, the governor was promoted to a high position in the capital.
  • 生恋姬，不肯偕行 - The young man was attached to Ji and did not want to go with him.
  • 而客囊垂罄 - But the guest's purse was nearly empty.
  • 益不能厌媪所欲 - He could no longer satisfy the old woman's desires.
  • 平日妒生者又从而交谤于媪 - Those who had envied the young man in the past joined in criticizing him to the old woman.
  • 媪益嗛之 - The old woman grew more resentful.
  • 尝拒不令与姬见 - She often refused to let him see Ji.
  • 姬私谓生曰 - Ji privately told the young man.
  • 始君为太守客 - At first, you were the governor's guest.
  • 凡事得恣所欲 - You could do whatever you wanted.
  • 媪尝敢怒而不敢言 - The old woman used to dare to be angry but not to speak out.
  • 今已矣 - But that is over now.
  • 平日妒君者皆欲报怨 - Those who envied you in the past all want to repay their grievances.
  • 媪日来颜色 - The old woman's demeanor.
  • 君当窥之 - You should observe it.
  • 脱不早见几 - If you do not act quickly.
  • 且不免于祸 - You will not escape disaster.
  • 因脱金缠臂一双纳生腕 - She then took off a pair of golden bracelets and put them on the young man's wrist.
  • 又赠白金一铤 - And gave him a piece of white gold.
  • 趣令速去 - Urging him to leave quickly.
  • 妾年方十八 - I am only eighteen.
  • 君方二十有二 - You are twenty-two.
  • 请待君三年 - Please wait for you for three years.
  • 以补司勋之恨 - To make up for the regret of the official.
  • 尔时齿各未衰 - At that time, our teeth have not yet decayed.
  • 为欢有日 - We will have days of happiness.
  • 以君才华门第 - Given your talent and family background.
  • 何患不出人头地 - Why worry about not standing out.
  • 好自为之 - Take good care of yourself.
  • 莫怪妾此时太恝也 - Do not blame me for being so indifferent now.
  • 千万珍重自爱 - Take good care of yourself.
  • 生泣听姬言 - The young man listened to Ji's words with tears.
  • 一语一首肯 - Agreeing with each word.
  • 不得已，挥泪而别 - Reluctantly, he wiped away his tears and said goodbye.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太守 - Refers to a governor, a high-ranking official in ancient China.
  • 京卿 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the capital, indicating a significant promotion.
  • 司勋 - Refers to an official responsible for rewards and honors, suggesting a past disappointment in this regard.
  • 金缠臂 - Refers to a type of jewelry, specifically a pair of golden bracelets.
  • 白金 - Refers to silver, a valuable metal used as currency.
  • 出人头地 - A common idiom meaning to stand out or excel, often used to describe someone who achieves great success.
  • 恝 - Refers to a state of indifference or detachment, often used to describe a person who is emotionally distant.
  • 珍重自爱 - Refers to the act of taking good care of oneself, often used as a farewell message.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 乃将白金略偿宿逋 - Means 'then used white gold to roughly settle old debts,' indicating a payment made to clear debts.
  • 趁舟旋楚 - Means 'took a boat to return to Chu,' referring to traveling by boat back to the state of Chu.
  • 比至汉皋 - Means 'when he arrived at Hanshui,' indicating a specific location.
  • 计距家仅百馀里 - Means 'calculated that he was only about a hundred miles from home,' showing the distance from his destination.
  • 以无资赁车 - Means 'because he had no money to hire a carriage,' explaining the reason for his mode of travel.
  • 又无傔从 - Means 'and had no attendants or companions,' indicating he traveled alone.
  • 襆被徒行 - Means 'wrapped his bedding and walked on foot,' describing his simple travel arrangements.
  • 日暮失路 - Means 'at dusk, lost his way,' indicating a time and situation of disorientation.
  • 寄宿邮亭 - Means 'stayed overnight in a post station,' describing a place of temporary lodging.
  • 跋涉劳顿 - Means 'after a long and tiring journey,' indicating physical exhaustion.
  • 一偃仰便已熟睡 - Means 'as soon as he lay down, he fell into a deep sleep,' describing a state of extreme fatigue.
  • 平明 - Means 'at dawn,' indicating the time of day.
  • 见巨蟒僵死身旁 - Means 'saw a huge python lying dead beside him,' describing a startling discovery.
  • 大惊，不知何故 - Means 'was greatly alarmed and did not know why,' expressing confusion and fear.
  • 俄顷 - Means 'in a short while,' indicating a brief period of time.
  • 土人毕至 - Means 'local people all arrived,' describing a gathering of locals.
  • 讶问姓名 - Means 'were surprised and asked for his name,' indicating their reaction.
  • 多有知者 - Means 'many of them knew him,' suggesting his recognition among the locals.
  • 群谓此蟒伤人甚多 - Means 'they said that this python had injured many people,' explaining the danger posed by the python.
  • 方苦无计诛除 - Means 'they were struggling to find a way to eliminate it,' indicating their efforts to deal with the problem.
  • 今忽就歼 - Means 'now it has suddenly been killed,' describing the unexpected outcome.
  • 君身有何宝物 - Means 'what valuable item do you have on your body?' asking about the cause of the python's death.
  • 生言无之 - Means 'he said he had none,' responding to the question.
  • 群不信 - Means 'they did not believe him,' indicating skepticism.
  • 试展其被 - Means 'they tried to unfold his blanket,' describing their action.
  • 见巾裹一物，大于鸡卵 - Means 'saw an object wrapped in a cloth, larger than a chicken's egg,' describing the item.
  • 有识者曰 - Means 'a knowledgeable person said,' introducing the explanation.
  • 此雄黄弹也 - Means 'this is a ball of realgar,' identifying the object.
  • 君有此至宝，岂不自知也耶 - Means 'you have such a precious item, how could you not know about it?' questioning his awareness.
  • 先是，姬每于暑月，辄患虫蚁 - Means 'previously, his wife often suffered from insects during the hot months,' describing a past issue.
  • 有漂水药商赠姬此物 - Means 'a water merchant gave this item to his wife,' explaining the origin of the item.
  • 置诸枕畔 - Means 'placed it beside her pillow,' describing where the item was kept.
  • 凡虿蠍及蝇蚋蜰蚤之属，果尽灭迹 - Means 'all scorpions, mosquitoes, flies, and fleas were completely eliminated,' describing the item's effectiveness.
  • 生无事尝取把玩 - Means 'he sometimes took it out to play with when he had nothing to do,' describing his habit.
  • 别时无意携裹巾中 - Means 'when he left, he accidentally carried it wrapped in a cloth,' explaining how it ended up with him.
  • 因识者见问，诡称祖遗 - Means 'because the knowledgeable person asked, he falsely claimed it was inherited from his ancestors,' describing his response.
  • 向实不知何用 - Means 'he had actually never known its use,' indicating his lack of knowledge.
  • 其人具言其宝贵难得 - Means 'the person explained its great value and rarity,' providing information about the item.
  • 群争传观，咋舌称异 - Means 'they all competed to pass it around and looked at it with amazement,' describing their reaction.
  • 爰共将死蟒剥皮刳肉 - Means 'they then collectively skinned and cut open the dead python,' describing their actions.
  • 刮鳞解骨，共得大小珠无算 - Means 'they scraped off the scales and removed the bones, and obtained countless pearls of various sizes,' describing the results.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遂以归生 - Ambiguous phrase; could mean 'he then took her back to his home' or 'he then brought her back to his home.'
  • 并各具酒食，延生至家 - Describes hospitality; 'and each prepared food and drink, inviting him to their home.'
  • 小住数日，以车送归 - Describes a brief visit followed by a return trip; 'stayed for a few days, and then sent him back by carriage.'
  • 计已逾约，于无人时，追忆坠欢，不禁潸焉出涕 - Describes a period exceeding an agreement and subsequent emotional distress; 'the time had already exceeded the agreed period, and when no one was around, he recalled past joys, and could not help but shed tears.'
  • 会有事过洞庭，猝遭暴风 - Describes a sudden encounter with a storm; 'happened to pass through Dongting Lake, and suddenly encountered a fierce storm.'
  • 适巡抚某公之任楚南，官舻势在危急 - Describes a perilous situation; 'just happened to be on a mission to the southern part of Chu, and the official boat was in grave danger.'
  • 某公见满湖客舟漂覆殆尽，惟生舟安稳无恙 - Describes a widespread disaster and the protagonist's safety; 'the official saw all the boats in the lake capsizing almost completely, but only the protagonist's boat remained safe and sound.'
  • 命材官持刺召生至，叙及先世，固有世谊 - Describes a historical connection; 'ordered a clerk to bring the protagonist to him, talked about their ancestors, and there was a long-standing friendship.'
  • 因问生有何术，令舟无患，生殊茫然 - Describes the protagonist's confusion; 'asked the protagonist what method he used to make the boat safe, and the protagonist was completely at a loss.'
  • 此诚希世之珍，无怪涉险不惊也 - Praises the pearl's value; 'this is truly a rare treasure of the world, it's no wonder you were not afraid of the danger.'
  • 生始恍然 - Describes the protagonist's realization; 'the protagonist suddenly realized.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 额上一珠大如鱼目 - Describes a large pearl on the protagonist's forehead, significant in the story.
  • 世谊 - Refers to a long-standing friendship or alliance between families, important in Chinese culture.
  • 定风珠 - Mythical object in Chinese folklore that can calm storms, symbolizing power and protection.

Proper Nouns

  • 归生 - Specific person, possibly a woman, taken back to the protagonist's home.
  • 某公 - Specific official, whose name is not mentioned.
  • 楚南 - Southern part of Chu, a historical region in China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以针黹佐婶食用 - Means 'helps her aunt with needlework to support the household,' where '针黹' refers to sewing and embroidery.
  • 相依为命 - Means 'rely on each other for survival,' indicating a close and dependent relationship.
  • 媒妁议婚 - Refers to arranged marriages facilitated by matchmakers.

  • 里有某公子 - Refers to a young nobleman from the same village.
  • 素渔于色 - Means 'has always been after women,' indicating a man who is known for his pursuit of women.
  • 偶见柿姑貌，艳之 - Means 'saw the beauty of Shigugu and was attracted to her,' where '艳' means to be attracted or enamored.
  • 冒托瓜葛亲，时来相挑 - Means 'pretended to be related and came to court her,' where '瓜葛' means distant kinship.

  • 某计贫士女当可以利诱 - Means 'he thought that a poor scholar's daughter could be tempted with gifts,' indicating his plan to use material gifts to win her over.
  • 瞷婶他出，特华服而往 - Means 'waited until her aunt left and then went in fine clothes,' where '瞷' means to wait for an opportunity.
  • 柿姑见某来，遽合其户 - Means 'Shigugu saw him coming and quickly closed the door,' where '遽' means quickly or hastily.
  • 某脱缠臂金自窗掷 - Means 'he threw a bracelet through the window,' where '缠臂金' refers to a bracelet.
  • 汝卿谁？谁是汝卿！ - Means 'Who are you calling me? Who are you calling yours!' indicating her anger and rejection.
  • 汝以此为好物，盍将去献汝母，固不失为孝。不则给汝姊若妹。似此龌龊物，休污我肉也！ - Means 'If you think this is a good thing, why not give it to your mother as a gift, which would make you a good son. Otherwise, give it to your sisters. Such a dirty thing should not defile my flesh!' This is a strong rebuke and rejection of his offer.
  • 某知不可犯，拾取衔恨而去 - Means 'He knew he could not force her, so he picked up the bracelet and left in anger,' where '衔恨' means to harbor resentment.
  • 婶归，柿姑且哭且诉 - Means 'When her aunt returned, Shigugu cried and told her what happened,' indicating her emotional state and the recounting of the incident.

  • 龌龊 - Refers to something dirty or impure, often used to express disgust or disapproval.

  • 何敢孟浪 - Means 'how dare we act rashly,' expressing caution.
  • 笑曰 - Means 'she laughed,' indicating the mother's reaction.
  • 吾意已决 - Means 'my mind is made up,' showing the mother's determination.
  • 此后一切礼节，仍旧从俭 - Means 'from now on, all the rituals will still be kept simple,' indicating a preference for simplicity.
  • 吾不汝苛也 - Means 'I will not be overly strict with you,' showing leniency.
  • 迫于婶命 - Means 'under pressure from the aunt's command,' indicating Xingsheng's compliance.

  • 别无长物 - Means 'there was nothing else of value,' emphasizing the lack of possessions.

  • 笑而不言 - Means 'smiled and did not speak,' indicating Xingsheng's non-committal response.

  • 妾亦不怨 - Means 'I do not resent it,' showing the daughter's acceptance.
  • 男儿不读书，究无长进 - Means 'if a man does not read books, he will not make much progress,' emphasizing the importance of education.
  • 君果肯下帷 - Means 'if you truly study diligently,' expressing hope for Xingsheng's commitment to learning.
  • 妾判劳十指，亦不至冻馁 - Means 'I will work hard with my hands, and we will not suffer from cold or hunger,' showing the daughter's willingness to support him.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 发义财 - Refers to the practice of donating charity funds, which was a common way to demonstrate social status and benevolence in traditional Chinese society.
  • 下帷 - A metaphor for diligent study, derived from the story of Dong Zhongshu, who studied so intensely that he hung a curtain to block out distractions.
  • 亲迎 - The traditional Chinese wedding custom where the groom personally goes to the bride's home to fetch her.

Proper Nouns

  • 柿姑 - The name of the daughter.
  • 婶 - The mother or aunt.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 当另犒赏 - Means 'shall be rewarded separately,' suggesting a promise of future recompense.
  • 如妄恣鱼肉 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'if one acts recklessly and cruelly,' likely referring to abuse of power or authority.
  • 九阍虽遥 - '九阍' refers to the nine gates of heaven or the celestial realm, '虽遥' means 'though distant,' suggesting a warning that even from afar, consequences will follow.
  • 勿谓我女流不能披发上叩也 - A strong statement meaning 'do not think that as a woman I cannot cut off my hair and ascend to make a plea,' indicating a determination to take extreme action if necessary.
  • 初县役所望甚奢 - Means 'initially, the county official had great expectations,' suggesting a high level of anticipation or hope.
  • 今见柿姑容光照人 - '容光照人' means 'her appearance is radiant and captivating,' indicating a striking presence.
  • 且复语言刚决 - Means 'and her words are firm and decisive,' suggesting a strong and resolute character.
  • 料非善弱 - Means 'she is not a weak or good-natured person,' implying a strong and potentially confrontational personality.
  • 只得唯唯听命 - Means 'can only nod in obedience,' indicating submission or compliance.

  • 待至十日 - Means 'after ten days,' marking a specific time frame.
  • 更不能耐 - Means 'could no longer bear it,' indicating growing frustration or impatience.
  • 乃折柬遍招绅衿 - Means 'she then sent invitations to all the gentry and scholars,' indicating a broad call to action.
  • 肆筵设席 - Means 'spread out the tables and set up the feast,' indicating a grand banquet.
  • 席罢 - Means 'after the banquet,' marking the end of the feast.
  • 柿姑于屏后白所求 - Means 'Miss Persimmon whispered her request behind the screen,' indicating a private or discreet communication.
  • 众人彼此相视 - Means 'the people looked at each other,' suggesting a moment of hesitation or uncertainty.
  • 默无一语 - Means 'silent and without a word,' indicating a lack of response.
  • 柿姑知众无能为助 - Means 'Miss Persimmon knew that the people could not help,' indicating a realization of futility.
  • 不禁浩然长叹 - Means 'could not help but sigh deeply,' indicating a profound sense of disappointment or frustration.
  • 含泪归房 - Means 'returned to her room with tears,' indicating emotional distress.
  • 乃自走笔具词为夫辨冤 - Means 'she then wrote a petition to clear her husband's name,' indicating a proactive effort to defend her husband.
  • 翌辰 - Means 'the next day,' marking a specific time frame.
  • 遣仆赍呈邑宰 - Means 'sent a servant to present it to the county magistrate,' indicating the delivery of the petition.
  • 中有联云 - Means 'there were couplets that said,' indicating the inclusion of poetic or literary elements.
  • 将错就错 - Means 'to follow a mistake with another mistake,' suggesting a continuation of error.
  • 参乎几误杀人 - Means 'Zengzi, how many people have you mistakenly killed?' referring to a famous Confucian scholar, Zengzi (曾子), and suggesting a question about the consequences of mistakes.
  • 以讹传讹 - Means 'to spread rumors or misinformation,' indicating the propagation of false information.
  • 回也岂真霸产 - Means 'Ruizi, could you truly be a bandit?' referring to Ruizi (回也), a disciple of Confucius, and suggesting a question about the nature of the accusation.
  • 宰见词叹赏 - Means 'the magistrate saw the petition and praised it,' indicating a positive reaction.
  • 立提生面讯 - Means 'immediately summoned the man for questioning,' indicating a swift action.
  • 既问 - Means 'after asking,' marking a specific point in the interrogation.
  • 汝同乡有蒋九成上舍 - Means 'do you have a fellow townsman named Jiang Jiucheng, who is a scholar?' indicating a question about a specific person.
  • 曾识其人否 - Means 'have you met him before?' indicating a question about familiarity.
  • 蒋上舍即生外舅 - Means 'Jiang Jiucheng is the man's father-in-law,' indicating a familial relationship.
  • 宰改容起立 - Means 'the magistrate changed his expression and stood up,' indicating a shift in attitude.
  • 然则汝妻即上舍之女 - Means 'then your wife is the daughter of the scholar,' indicating a revelation of the relationship.
  • 无怪立言得体也 - Means 'no wonder her words are so appropriate,' indicating a justification for the petition's effectiveness.
  • 遂以柿姑所上之词付生阅之 - Means 'then the magistrate gave the man the petition to read,' indicating the sharing of the document.
  • 即日出生于狱 - Means 'the man was released from prison that day,' indicating a swift resolution.
  • 邑宰某公，固长白人，父官都统 - Means 'the county magistrate, Mr. So-and-so, is originally from Changbai, and his father held the position of Duting (都统),' indicating background information about the magistrate.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 顧小人多反复 - Common suspicion of untrustworthy individuals in classical Chinese literature.
  • 賢妹，前詞謂回也霸產 - Yan Hui (颜回) is a famous disciple of Confucius known for his virtue and poverty. The phrase '回也霸產' suggests a contradiction in his character.
  • 典出何書 - Reference to a specific classical text, likely the *Analects* (論語) or another Confucian work.
  • 參乎殺人 - Reference to a specific passage in the *Analects* where Confucius discusses Yan Hui's character.
  • 拾塵故事 - Old, possibly obscure story being referenced.

  • 將‘霸產’改‘盜食’二字 - Change in wording to avoid a contradiction or make the statement more coherent.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 里乘子曰 - Classical narrative device meaning 'the storyteller said' or 'it is said by the one who records the tale.'
  • 幸生待还遗金 - Describes a situation where a person named '幸生' waits to return lost money.
  • 柿姑掷还缠臂金 - Describes an event where '柿姑' throws back a bracelet (缠臂金) that was found.
  • 一介一贞 - Refers to a man's integrity (一介) and a woman's chastity (一贞).
  • 是天生嘉耦 - Means 'this is a naturally good match,' implying that the couple is destined to be together.
  • 义财必待柿姑掘发 - Suggests that righteous wealth must be revealed by '柿姑' through some action (掘发, digging up).
  • 天赏生之介，实天赏女之贞也 - Implies that heaven rewards '幸生' for his integrity, which is actually rewarding '柿姑' for her chastity.
  • 婶为幸生所愚 - Means 'the aunt was deceived by '幸生.''
  • 生初固无愚婶之心 - Indicates that '幸生' originally had no intention to deceive his aunt.
  • 婶忽启疑团，迫成夫妇者，亦天使之，非生愚之也 - Suggests that the aunt suddenly raised doubts, leading to the marriage, which was also willed by heaven, not caused by '幸生's' deception.
  • 顾生闻婶穷问，但含糊不辨是非，即此足见其黠，固无足怪 - Describes how '幸生' responded to his aunt's questioning in a vague manner, showing his cleverness, which is not surprising.
  • 睹柿姑掘财，犹诡答不肯质言，则黠而近于诈矣，吾无取焉 - States that when '幸生' saw '柿姑' uncovering the treasure, he still gave a deceptive answer, showing that his cleverness borders on deceit, which is not acceptable.
  • 柿姑天性英爽 - Describes '柿姑' as naturally bright and spirited.
  • 斥责某公子与面命邑役之言 - Refers to '柿姑' rebuking a certain young nobleman and instructing the local officials.
  • 破悭囊以助膏火 - Means 'she broke open a miser's bag to help with the cost of education.'
  • 怨以德遇之，立论尤为谛当 - Suggests that '柿姑' met the miser's resentment with kindness and her argument was particularly sound.
  • 健妇持门户，终胜一丈夫 - A proverb meaning 'a strong woman managing the household is better than a weak man.'
  • 得妇若此，即不掘义财，吾决其家道必昌也 - Indicates that even without uncovering righteous wealth, having such a wife would ensure the family's prosperity.
  • 彼公子图人，几反自坐，输金获免，犹属幸事 - Describes how the young nobleman tried to harm others but almost ended up harming himself, paying money to avoid punishment, which is still fortunate.
  • 祸人转以福人，射影含沙，徒劳心计 - Suggests that those who harm others often end up benefiting others, and plotting against others is futile.
  • 纨少年，当奉为殷鉴 - Advises young nobles to take this as a warning.
  • 邑宰某公，惓惓不忘师谊，存心甚厚 - Describes a local official who deeply remembers his teacher's friendship and has a kind heart.
  • 适然来宰斯土，意者亦彼苍预为位置欤 - Suggests that the official's arrival might have been predestined by heaven.
  • 不然，何其巧也 - Asks if it is not a coincidence, implying that it is indeed predestined.

  • 彼苍 - A term for heaven or the sky.
  • 师谊 - Refers to the relationship between a teacher and student.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 是以 - Conjunction meaning 'therefore' or 'thus,' used to introduce a conclusion based on preceding circumstances.

  • 先是 - Temporal marker meaning 'previously' or 'at that time,' setting the scene for the preceding narrative.
  • 女郎偕一童游观中 - Means 'a young lady accompanied by a child was visiting a place of interest,' describing the initial situation.

  • 雏女 - A young girl, often used to describe a vulnerable or innocent person.
  • 承平世宙 - A peaceful world, often used to describe a time of stability and prosperity.
  • 弱息 - Vulnerable children, often used to describe those in need of protection.
  • 光天化日 - Broad daylight, often used to describe a time when actions should be visible and just.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 言讫 - Indicates the completion of a speech or statement.
  • 攘臂跃入人丛 - Describes a bold action of raising one's arms and leaping into a crowd.
  • 横身要截 - Means to cut across the body, indicating a physical barrier or intervention.
  • 厉声叱曰 - Means shouted sternly, indicating a commanding and forceful tone.
  • 鼠子不得无礼 - A derogatory term used to insult the crowd, emphasizing the speaker's disdain.
  • 逢场作剧 - Means to act out a scene, often used to describe playful or theatrical behavior.
  • 吾侪 - Means we or us, referring to a group of people.
  • 若何人 - Means who are you, a rhetorical question challenging the speaker's authority.
  • 敢败乃公事 - Means dare to ruin our business, a threat or challenge to the speaker's actions.
  • 硬语牴牾 - Means harsh words and arguments, indicating a confrontational exchange.
  • 遽前 - Means quickly stepped forward, describing an impulsive action.
  • 以掌掴君面 - Means to slap the speaker's face with a palm, a physical attack.
  • 佝身疾出腋下 - Describes a quick and agile movement, bending the body to move swiftly.
  • 反掌搏其背 - Means to turn the palm and strike the back, a physical counterattack.
  • 以趾踆之颠 - Means to kick the head with the toes, another physical counterattack.
  • 连踣四五人 - Means knocked down four or five people in a row, indicating a series of successful attacks.
  • 馀乃不敢继进 - Means the rest did not dare to follow, indicating the crowd's retreat.
  • 鸟兽散 - Means dispersed like birds and beasts, a metaphor for a chaotic and rapid dispersal.
  • 旁观如堵墙 - Means onlookers like a wall, describing a dense crowd of spectators.
  • 初以君文弱卤莽 - Means initially thought the speaker was weak and reckless, indicating initial perceptions.
  • 幸君胜 - Means fortunately, the speaker won, indicating a positive outcome.
  • 欢声雷动 - Means cheers like thunder, describing a loud and enthusiastic response.
  • 色喜耳语 - Means smiling and whispering, indicating a positive reaction.
  • 窃谓 - Means whispering to each other, indicating private conversations.
  • 啧啧称道不置 - Means continuously praising, indicating ongoing admiration.
  • 同人从而虑之 - Means friends then considered, indicating a group discussion.
  • 但快心于一时 - Means only to satisfy oneself for a moment, indicating a short-term satisfaction.
  • 懋建义举 - Means to undertake noble deeds, indicating a commitment to righteous actions.
  • 群儿溃窜 - Means the children fled in panic, indicating a chaotic retreat.
  • 怒形于色 - Means anger showed on their faces, indicating visible emotions.
  • 必号召以图报复 - Means they will surely call for revenge, indicating a threat of retaliation.
  • 倘众寡不敌 - Means if the numbers are not equal, indicating a concern about numerical disparity.

  • 为德不可不卒 - Means to complete a good deed, indicating a commitment to follow through.
  • 毋短气 - Means do not lose courage, indicating a call to maintain resolve.
  • 毋贰心 - Means do not have divided loyalties, indicating a call to remain united.
  • 当同卫送两小归 - Means to escort the two girls home together, indicating a plan of action.
  • 纵与若曹遇 - Means even if we encounter you, indicating a hypothetical scenario.
  • 共击无不利 - Means we will strike without fail, indicating a confident prediction of success.
  • 佥曰 - Means all agreed, indicating a consensus.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 里乘子曰 - Formal phrase indicating the speaker or author, similar to 'The Master said' or 'The Author says.'
  • 肆力于诗古文词 - Literally 'exerted effort on poetry, ancient prose, and literary discourse,' meaning he devoted himself to these literary forms.
  • 相得甚欢 - Literally 'found each other very delightful,' meaning they got along very well.
  • 手巨觥拈髭纵谈往事 - Literally 'took a large cup in hand, twirled his mustache, and freely talked about past events,' meaning he drank and reminisced.
  • 精悍之色，犹见眉宇 - Literally 'the sharp and vigorous expression still showed in his brows and eyes,' meaning his demeanor was still lively and strong.
  • 阔别且有十馀年矣 - Literally 'we have been apart for more than ten years,' meaning they have not seen each other for over a decade.
  • 每一忆及之，殊令人神往于苏长公遇方山子时也 - Literally 'every time I recall it, it really makes me long for the time when Su Changle met Fang Shanzi,' meaning it brings a strong sense of nostalgia and admiration.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 李申耆先生 - Refers to Li Shengqi, a respected scholar or teacher.

  • 虞姬墓 - Refers to the tomb of Yu Ji, a famous concubine of Xiang Yu from the Chu-Han Contention.
  • 燕许 - Refers to Yan Shigu and Xu Jingzong, two famous officials and writers from the Tang Dynasty known for their excellent writing skills.
  • 骈文 - Refers to parallel prose, a style of writing characterized by balanced sentences and antithetical couplets.
  • 苏长公 - Refers to Su Dongpo (Su Shi), a renowned poet and writer from the Song Dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 美人乃前敛衽曰 - 美人 (beautiful lady) is a respectful term for a woman. 敛衽 (liǎn rèn) means to gather one's sleeves, a gesture of respect or humility. 乃前 (nǎi qián) means 'then stepped forward.'
  • 妾与先生固有文字因缘 - 妾 (qiè) is a humble term for a woman addressing a man. 固有 (gù yǒu) means 'already has.' 文字因缘 (wén zì yīn yuán) refers to a predestined connection through writing.
  • 故涉嫌就教，休得惊怪 - 故 (gù) means 'therefore.' 涉嫌 (xiàng yí) means 'suspected of' or 'alleged to be.' 就教 (jiù jiào) means 'to seek instruction.' 休得惊怪 (xiū dé jīng guài) is a polite way to say 'do not be surprised.'
  • 予亟答拜曰 - 予 (yǔ) is a humble term for 'I.' 亟 (jí) means 'hastily' or 'quickly.' 答拜 (dá bài) means 'to return a bow.'
  • 一介陬生，伏处穷巷 - 一介 (yī jiè) means 'a mere.' 陬生 (zōu shēng) refers to a person from a remote or humble background. 伏处 (fú chù) means 'to live in seclusion.' 穷巷 (qióng xiàng) means 'a poor alley.'
  • 不省何处得侍天人 - 不省 (bù xǐng) means 'do not know.' 何处 (hé chǔ) means 'where.' 得侍 (dé shì) means 'to be in the presence of.' 天人 (tiān rén) means 'heavenly being' or 'immortal.'
  • 所谓文字因缘，傋瞀莫解 - 所谓 (suǒ wèi) means 'so-called' or 'as for.' 傋瞀 (mào mào) means 'confused' or 'bewildered.' 莫解 (mò jiě) means 'cannot understand.'
  • 请明谕其旨，以开愚窦 - 请 (qǐng) means 'please.' 明谕 (míng yù) means 'clearly explain.' 其旨 (qí zhǐ) means 'its meaning.' 以开愚窦 (yǐ kāi yú dòu) means 'to open the eyes of the ignorant.'
  • 妾乃西楚虞姬是也 - 乃 (nǎi) means 'is.' 西楚 (xī chǔ) refers to the region of Western Chu. 虞姬 (yú jī) is the name of a famous beauty and concubine of Xiang Yu, a historical figure.
  • 前朱使君泽及枯骨 - 前 (qián) means 'former.' 朱使君 (zhū shǐ jūn) is a respectful title for a local official named Zhu. 泽及枯骨 (zé jí kū gǔ) means 'blessed even the dead.'
  • 知碑文出自椽笔 - 知 (zhī) means 'know.' 碑文 (bēi wén) means 'inscription on a stele.' 出自 (chū zì) means 'originates from.' 椽笔 (chuán bǐ) means 'a skilled writer.'
  • 崇论伟议 - 崇论 (chóng lùn) means 'highly esteemed discourse.' 伟议 (wěi yì) means 'great arguments.'
  • 使妾读之，千年幽愤，顿为一泄 - 使 (shǐ) means 'make.' 读之 (dú zhī) means 'to read it.' 千年幽愤 (qiān nián yōu fèn) means 'thousand-year-old hidden resentment.' 顿为一泄 (dùn wéi yī xiè) means 'instantly released.'
  • 昨同戚妹往东海寿上元夫人 - 昨 (zuó) means 'yesterday.' 同 (tóng) means 'with.' 戚妹 (qī mèi) means 'sister.' 往 (wǎng) means 'to go.' 东海 (dōng hǎi) refers to the Eastern Sea. 寿上元夫人 (shòu shàng yuán fū rén) means 'to celebrate the birthday of the Lady of the Upper Yuan.'
  • 过我王庙宫 - 过 (guò) means 'visited.' 我王庙宫 (wǒ wáng miào gōng) means 'my king's temple and palace.'
  • 顺道一讯起居 - 顺道 (shùn dào) means 'on the way.' 一讯 (yī xùn) means 'to inquire.' 起居 (qǐ jū) means 'well-being.'
  • 将归瑶池 - 将 (jiāng) means 'about to.' 归 (guī) means 'return.' 瑶池 (yáo chí) refers to the Jade Pool, a mythical place in Daoist tradition.
  • 稔知君舟杈此 - 稔知 (rěn zhī) means 'know well.' 君舟 (jūn zhōu) means 'your boat.' 杈此 (chà cǐ) means 'anchored here.'
  • 特诣谢巨制耳 - 特 (tè) means 'especially.' 诣 (yì) means 'to visit.' 谢 (xiè) means 'to thank.' 巨制 (jù zhì) means 'great work.' 耳 (ěr) is a particle indicating a conclusion.
  • 予憬然逊曰 - 予 (yǔ) means 'I.' 憬然 (jǐng rán) means 'suddenly enlightened.' 逊 (xùn) means 'to bow' or 'to humbly say.'
  • 仙姬贞情烈魄 - 仙姬 (xiān jī) means 'immortal lady.' 贞情 (zhēn qíng) means 'loyal feelings.' 烈魄 (liè pò) means 'brave spirit.'
  • 愧咫闻肤见 - 愧 (kuì) means 'ashamed.' 咫闻 (zhǐ wén) means 'limited knowledge.' 肤见 (fū jiàn) means 'superficial understanding.'
  • 不能揄扬万一 - 不能 (bù néng) means 'cannot.' 揄扬 (yú yáng) means 'to praise.' 万一 (wàn yī) means 'even a little.'
  • 辱挂齿颊 - 辱 (rǔ) means 'to be honored.' 挂齿颊 (guà chǐ jiá) means 'to mention.'
  • 反增汗颜 - 反 (fǎn) means 'instead.' 增 (zēng) means 'increase.' 汗颜 (hàn yán) means 'feel ashamed.'
  • 先生勿过撝谦 - 先生 (xiān shēng) is a respectful term for a man. 勿 (wù) means 'do not.' 过 (guò) means 'excessively.' 撝谦 (huī qiān) means 'to be modest.'
  • 文信必传 - 文信 (wén xìn) means 'reliable writing.' 必传 (bì chuán) means 'will be passed down.'
  • 但承褒誉过情 - 但 (dàn) means 'only.' 承 (chéng) means 'receive.' 褒誉 (bāo yù) means 'praise.' 过情 (guò qíng) means 'excessive.'
  • 未免感极生愧 - 未免 (wèi miǎn) means 'cannot help but.' 感极 (gǎn jí) means 'feel deeply.' 生愧 (shēng kuì) means 'feel ashamed.'
  • 妾尤喜叙次论断 - 妾 (qiè) means 'I.' 尤 (yóu) means 'especially.' 喜 (xǐ) means 'like.' 叙次 (xù cì) means 'to arrange.' 论断 (lùn duàn) means 'to judge.'
  • 多与当年情事符合 - 多 (duō) means 'many.' 与 (yǔ) means 'and.' 当年 (dāng nián) means 'that year.' 情事 (qíng shì) means 'events.' 符合 (fú hé) means 'consistent with.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 西楚虞姬 - 虞姬 (yú jī) is the name of a famous beauty and concubine of Xiang Yu, a historical figure.
  • 朱使君 - 朱使君 (zhū shǐ jūn) is a respectful title for a local official named Zhu.
  • 瑶池 - 瑶池 (yáo chí) refers to the Jade Pool, a mythical place in Daoist tradition.

Proper Nouns

  • 东海 - 东海 (dōng hǎi) refers to the Eastern Sea.
  • 上元夫人 - 上元夫人 (shàng yuán fū rén) is the Lady of the Upper Yuan.
  • 王庙宫 - 王庙宫 (wáng miào gōng) means 'my king's temple and palace.'
  • 瑶池 - 瑶池 (yáo chí) refers to the Jade Pool, a mythical place in Daoist tradition.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 垓下之战 - Historical battle where Liu Bang defeated Xiang Yu, marking the end of the Chu-Han Contention and the beginning of the Han Dynasty.

  • 匼匝 - Term describing a tight encirclement or surrounding.
  • 薄暮 - Refers to late afternoon or dusk.
  • 黑云如磐 - Vivid description of dark clouds heavy as a millstone.

  • 夜分 - Refers to midnight.
  • 楚歌四起 - Indicates the sound of Chu songs coming from all directions, suggesting the encirclement by enemy forces.
  • 拊髀垂涕 - Gesture of sorrow and regret, where one strokes their thigh and weeps.

  • 荷王厚遇 - Means to receive the king's generous treatment.
  • 自有以报 - Indicates having a way to repay.
  • 勿以妾为念 - Request not to worry about oneself.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 仙姬久归仙籍 - The fairy princess has long returned to her celestial registry; a poetic way of saying she has ascended to heaven.
  • 只合 - Should only or ought only, indicating a prescribed course of action.
  • 聊供判论 - Merely for judgment or commentary, suggesting a detached, analytical perspective.
  • 慎勿过事伤感 - Carefully avoid excessive grief, urging restraint in emotional expression.
  • 有损玉抱 - Harm the jade-like bosom, a metaphor for damaging one's refined nature or spirit.
  • 大节皭然 - The great virtue is pure and clear, a reference to maintaining moral integrity.
  • 薰莸相去 - The difference between fragrant and stinky, a metaphor for the distinction between good and bad qualities.
  • 何可以道里计哉 - How can it be measured in terms of distance, emphasizing the vast difference between good and bad.
  • 此是先生藻奖 - This is your praise, acknowledging the compliment.
  • 若曹所为 - What you people do, referring to the actions of the addressee and others.
  • 妾实羞之 - I truly feel ashamed of it, expressing personal disgrace.
  • 宁死不愿效也 - I would rather die than imitate it, a strong statement of refusal.
  • 未审仙姬当日毅然死别 - I am unsure if the fairy princess, when she died, was resolute in her decision to part ways, questioning the certainty of the event.
  • 后事犹能知悉否 - Whether she still knows the subsequent events, inquiring about her awareness of what happened after her death.
  • 妾身虽死 - Even though I am dead, indicating a continuation of consciousness after death.
  • 魂固在王左右 - My spirit remains by the king's side, suggesting a lingering presence.
  • 王见妾已死，号恸失声 - The king saw that I was dead and wailed in grief, describing the king's reaction.
  • 恐人跆藉妾尸 - Fearing that people might trample on my body, expressing concern for the body's dignity.
  • 命军校裹以毳旃 - Ordered the military officers to wrap my body in a fur blanket, detailing the burial process.
  • 舁瘗浅土 - Carried and buried in shallow soil, describing the burial method.
  • 独骑决围而走 - Rode alone through the encirclement and fled, depicting the king's escape.
  • 王所乘骓马 - The king's steed, the zui horse, referring to the king's horse.
  • 究竟何若 - What exactly is it like, inquiring about the nature of the horse.
  • 先三日前 - Three days before, indicating a specific time.
  • 前蹄忽踅 - The front hooves suddenly turned, describing an unusual behavior of a horse.
  • 不祥 - Unlucky, expressing a negative omen.
  • 后所乘似骓实非 - The subsequent horse, although resembling a zui, is not one, clarifying the nature of the horse.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大节 - Maintaining moral integrity, a reference to the virtue of remaining pure and upright.
  • 薰莸 - Fragrant and stinky, a metaphor for good and bad qualities, respectively.
  • 道里 - Distance, used metaphorically to emphasize the vast difference between good and bad.
  • 藻奖 - Praise, a term for commendation or recognition.
  • 若曹 - You people, a term for a group of people, often used in a derogatory sense.
  • 妾 - I, a term used by women to refer to themselves.
  • 王 - King, a term for a ruler or monarch.

  • 毳旃 - Fur blanket, a type of blanket made from animal fur.
  • 舁瘗 - Carried and buried, a term for the act of carrying a body to its burial site.
  • 决围 - Through the encirclement, a term for breaking through a blockade or encirclement.
  • 骓马 - Zui horse, a type of horse, often associated with speed and strength.
  • 不祥 - Unlucky, a term for a negative omen or bad luck.
  • 前蹄忽踅 - The front hooves suddenly turned, a term for an unusual behavior of a horse.
  • 浅土 - Shallow soil, a term for the depth of the burial.
  • 独骑 - Rode alone, a term for riding a horse by oneself.
  • 决围而走 - Through the encirclement and fled, a term for breaking through a blockade and escaping.
  • 究竟 - Exactly, a term for a precise or definitive answer.
  • 何若 - What exactly is it like, a term for inquiring about the nature or characteristics of something.
  • 先三日前 - Three days before, a term for a specific time.
  • 后所乘 - The subsequent horse, a term for a horse that follows another.
  • 似骓实非 - Although resembling a zui, is not one, a term for clarifying the nature of something.
